item.title: 2026 Accounting Trends: AI, Sustainability & Regulatory Shifts
item.dc:creator: dan
item.content:encoded:
2025 was a banner year for accounting optimization, and 2026 looks ready to be the same. As your organization reflects, it’s time to embrace the technology trends that’ll define 2026.

While the year certainly had its challenges, organizations proactively responded by embracing flexibility and functionality to address sustainability. From examining operational processes to improving drivers of mission success, process optimization is defined for 2025 and is expected to be at the forefront in 2026.

As organizations work to be nimbler, they are embracing AI, automation, and the continued move to cloud accounting. So as your organization readies for the new year, let’s explore the top nonprofit and association accounting trends of 2026 and examine how to best position your organization for sustained success.

Key Accounting Trends for 2026

AI Adoption and Practical Use Cases

AI in accounting has moved from novelty to necessity, offering practical solutions that enhance speed and accuracy.

While only 21% of organizations used AI in the past calendar year, those that do found significant value in content generation, marketing, and analytics, and planned to increase their investment in AI, according to a Momentive Software survey.

As more AI tools are developed, organizations can use AI to reduce administrative work while assisting with tasks such as brainstorming new fundraising ideas, creating content templates to thank supporters, and summarizing essential statistics in an easy-to-communicate format.

Accountants can use AI to automatically categorize complex transactions, flag potential fraud or compliance risks, and perform predictive analytics to better forecast cash flow based on historical donor data.

Automation of Workflows and Reporting to Combat Talent Shortages

The persistent shortage of skilled finance professionals in the nonprofit sector continues to put pressure on existing teams. Organizations are responding by investing in upskilling current staff in new technologies such as AI and automation, and by leveraging integrated software to simplify workflows, making the financial management role more strategic and less administrative.

AI can help organizations fight talent shortages and decrease administrative costs. Some practical uses of AI include streamlining workflows for invoice processing, bank reconciliations, and routine grant reporting. This not only frees up finance teams to focus on strategy but also drastically reduces the potential for manual data entry errors.

Cloud-Based Accounting

Again, cloud-based accounting tools remain increasingly relevant. Several years of research show that organizations continue to migrate to cloud-based accounting software, and migrating to the cloud remains a major pillar of cloud accounting trends.

Cloud-based systems provide the flexibility needed for hybrid workforces and ensure data security and accessibility. The shift allows finance teams to access real-time data from any location, making quick pivots and scenario planning much simpler.

Cloud systems foster collaboration, offer new opportunities for information sharing through comprehensive dashboards and reports that facilitate exchange, and, when appropriately utilized, increase efficiency throughout an organization and improve visibility into operations.

Increased Demand for Real-Time Financial Visibility

The future of accounting is centered on real-time intelligence that makes stats accessible to everyone in an organization.

Executive leadership and boards are increasingly demanding immediate visibility into financial health, program efficacy, and fund utilization. This necessitates robust reporting tools that can instantly connect revenue and expenditure to mission outcomes.

When it comes to reporting and increasing visibility, your organization must embrace a solution that gives complete, at-a-glance visibility into your organization’s financial health. It helps leadership and the board stay updated on cash flow, key metrics, and provides the critical insights you need to make confident decisions—all from a single, intuitive screen.

Help your organization be more responsive in 2026. The Go-to Guide for Mission-driven Accounting covers the fundamentals of sound financial management and compliance, while positioning your organization for strategic growth.

Help your organization be more responsive in 2026.

The Go-to Guide for Mission-driven Accounting covers the fundamentals of sound financial management and compliance, while positioning your organization for strategic growth.

Download Now

Regulatory and Compliance Shifts

The regulatory landscape is set for continued evolution, placing greater demands on nonprofit and association organizations for transparency and detailed reporting. Keeping pace with these mandates requires continuous monitoring and investment in flexible accounting systems that can quickly adapt to new rules, ensuring your organization remains compliant and avoids potential penalties. Proactively addressing these shifts is essential for maintaining stakeholder trust and focusing resources on core mission delivery rather than reactive compliance efforts.

ESG and Sustainability Pressures

Growing focus on Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) factors will increasingly impact financial reporting, even within the nonprofit sector. Organizations will need to develop systems to track and report on non-financial metrics related to sustainability, demonstrating their commitment to broader societal impact beyond their immediate mission. This heightened scrutiny necessitates integrating ESG data collection into core accounting processes, allowing for transparent disclosure that satisfies donor and stakeholder expectations.

Data Security and Fraud Prevention

With the increased reliance on cloud-based systems and real-time data access, data security will become a paramount concern, especially as nonprofits handle sensitive donor and financial information. Using cloud-based systems with industry-standard security built in tools to prevent fraud is crucial.

Nonprofit Accounting Trends

Just as organizations pivoted to become nimbler in 2025, it’ll remain just as crucial in 2026. As we navigate through political and economic uncertainties, technological shifts, and changing donor behaviors, the need for data-driven insights and unified systems has never been more critical.

Momentive Software research shows that early technology adopters enjoy significant advantages across multiple performance metrics. These organizations are more likely to be very optimistic about their future and report stronger revenue growth, donor engagement, and operational efficiency.

The organizations that will thrive in the coming years aren’t necessarily those with the most significant budgets or longest histories, but rather those that strategically leverage technology, diversify funding, strengthen donor relationships, embrace innovation, and build collaborative networks.

Sustainability pressures

The nonprofit sector continues to face extraordinary challenges to sustainability. Revenues and donations are shrinking, and nonprofits nationwide are scrambling to find new funding streams and explore new revenue opportunities. In this uncertain economic climate, it’s more important than ever for nonprofits to think creatively and strategically about their budgeting efforts.

As we move into the new year, questions about the economy’s strength remain. When planning for uncertainty, nonprofit leaders should look back at what’s worked before, create scenarios for how the year could unfold, and develop separate plans and budgets for their most optimistic year, a slightly scaled-back year, and one that accounts for decreased revenues and donations.

Be prepared to pivot to other scenarios if your actuals aren’t meeting your projections, and don’t be afraid to try something new. The Guide to 2026 Proactive Budgeting is your secret weapon for moving beyond traditional budgeting and creating a fiscally sound financial roadmap for 2026 and beyond.

Revenue diversification

The top way to battle those sustainability challenges is through revenue diversification.

Trying new ways to reach existing donors and find new donors can help your nonprofit create new revenue sources. Peer-to-peer fundraising continues to be one of the fastest-growing ways for nonprofits to expand their reach and revenue. In fact, participation in the top 30 peer-to-peer fundraising programs grew 15.6% from 2022 to 2023, signaling that organizations of every size are seeing success by turning their supporters into active fundraisers.

Peer-to-peer fundraising empowers your most passionate advocates to raise money on your behalf by launching their own campaigns and sharing them with friends, family, and coworkers. It’s not just a modern fundraising trend—it’s a proven way to engage new audiences and build long-term donor relationships. It is key for organizations looking to diversify their revenue streams in the new year. But it’s not the only solution. When considering your 2026 projections, take a holistic view of your organization and examine if intradepartmental barriers contribute to inefficiency.

Fundraising and accounting barriers

Interdepartmental barriers prevent complete visibility into fiscal health, as organizations often use separate systems for accounting and fundraising. Even if unintentional, a barrier between these departments limits visibility into nonprofit operations and can contribute to errors with manual data entry.

Breaking down these barriers will be critical to overcoming uncertainty and having proper visibility into nonprofit health.

Robert Parkinson, CFO at Paterson, N.J.-based Turning Point, broke down a barrier in his organization by unifying his organization’s fundraising and accounting software.

“Any money that we don’t have to spend on extra administrative costs, extra systems, and even extra time can be dollars spent toward fulfilling the needs of our community,” Parkinson said. “We’re replacing time-consuming spreadsheets, calculations, and back and forth across people and teams with GiveSmart and MIP, the automation of data and transactions frees us up and is going to save us time and money that can go back into our treatment programs.”

As Parkinson demonstrates, breaking down this barrier increases efficiency by strategically unifying data, eliminating manual entry, streamlining reporting workflows, and aligning each department toward your organization’s most significant goal: accomplishing its mission.

Watch Unifying Accounting and Fundraising for Greater Mission Impact to see how MIP Fund Accounting and GiveSmart come together to bridge the gap between giving and accounting.

You’ll learn how integrated systems can transform the way your organization tracks donations, manages funds, and reports on impact.

Watch Now

The final piece of the puzzle to operational success in 2026 is embracing operational efficiency.

Association Accounting Trends for 2026

Associations face similar pressures as nonprofits in the coming year. While reliance on dues and events gives associations a unique financial profile, fluctuating member numbers and tightened professional budgets are leading to unstable member enrollment.

Similarly, association accounting trends demand adaptive forecasting and meticulous tracking of both dues and non-dues revenue to ensure financial stability amid fluctuating conditions.

Membership and Budget Modeling

Just like with nonprofits, success hinges on precision. Instead of static budgets, associations must prioritize fluidity and create a living, breathing document. Membership and dues forecasting based on anticipated churn, join rates, and tiered structures. Due to economic uncertainty, this must be paired with budgeting in fluctuating membership environments, requiring multiple scenario plans (optimistic, base, guardrail) to pre-determine cost controls and ensure financial resilience.

Members’ Changing Preferences

For years, a critical knowledge gap has plagued associations, leading directly to membership lapse. Associations underprioritize career services, yet members see them as a top priority.

Help your organization be more responsive in 2026.

The Go-to Guide for Mission-driven Accounting covers the fundamentals of sound financial management and compliance, while positioning your organization for strategic growth.

To retain and grow membership in 2026, associations need to cater to all members during the different stages of their career journeys.

Closing the Gap: Solving the Disconnect Around Career Prioritization examines the persistent gap between association offerings and member values, documents current and lapsed members' priorities, and explores why a one-size-fits-all approach is fueling churn across all professional stages.

Learn More

Growing Non-Dues Revenue Streams

Non-dues revenue streams require sophisticated tracking. Associations must differentiate and track income from both in-person and virtual components of hybrid program revenue. Furthermore, integration is key to accurately tracking all event-based and educational income, including certifications, registrations, and exhibit fees, ensuring every revenue source is measured against its true cost for accurate profitability reporting.

Preparing for the Future of Accounting

The coming year presents a mandate for mission-driven organizations: to achieve sustainable growth you must embrace operational efficiency and integrated technology.

Organizations that will thrive will move beyond static budgeting by implementing scenario planning and aggressively pursuing revenue diversification, using strategies like peer-to-peer fundraising. Crucially, success requires breaking down interdepartmental barriers by unifying fundraising and accounting systems to eliminate errors and gain the real-time financial visibility demanded by boards.

Ultimately, technological adoption, from cloud accounting and automation to exploring the practical uses of AI, will drive greater mission impact by freeing up administrative costs.

By proactively addressing these shifts, your organization can be fiscally sound, highly compliant, and ready to navigate any economic uncertainty.

FAQ

Ready to make 2026 your year? Discover how accounting software can help you simplify compliance and focus your resources entirely on your mission.

Get Started

item.title: Association Trends 2026: Membership, Revenue, AI & Growth Strategies
item.dc:creator: dan
item.content:encoded:
The past year was transformative for associations as they work to meet member needs and grow non-dues revenues to combat economic uncertainty. Now more than ever, associations must close critical gaps with their members to recruit, retain, and boost revenue. To navigate these challenges, the biggest association trends for 2026 are centered on optimization and efficiency. 

As associations work to be nimbler, they are embracing AI, automation, and technology, and more. So as your organization readies for the new year, let’s explore the top association trends for 2026 and examine how to best position your organization to maximize membership, revenue, technology, AI, and fundraising use.  

Ready to strengthen your strategy for the year ahead? Explore how Association Management Software helps organizations streamline operations, engage members, and build long-term sustainability.

Explore now

Key Challenges Facing Associations and Nonprofits in 2026 

As 2026 approaches, associations and nonprofits face a shifting landscape defined by economic uncertainty, policy changes, and shifting member expectations. Plus, many organizations are still overcoming funding disruptions and workforce constraints from previous years.  

Between sustainability and stability concerns, economic and policy uncertainty, and staffing shortages, it’s crucial for associations to prioritize streamlining administrative standards, and technical processes.  

Stability Concerns 

Pending and predicted executive orders may significantly negatively impact your key funding sources in 2026. How can you level out your budget and prepare for potential funding shortfalls? 

Diversifying revenue streams and driving non-dues revenue have already been crucial steps to ensure association success, and in today’s economy, they will become the lynchpin of success.  

Association Event Trends 

Yet, a tried and true mainstay of associations trends should still be the focus to overcome stability concerns in 2026: events will remain the foundation of stability in 2026. They are the top priority for 88% of associations.  

88%  

of organizations view events as their top focus in 2026

Source: Bridging the Gap: Aligning Association Professionals and Members for Success 

While large scale events are here to stay, an opportunity is emerging for the association to embrace smaller-scale regional events to complement major conferences, with some member segments, particularly Gen Z and specific sectors, such as technology/IT, expressing interest in these events. 

Events already are the foundation of revenue success, but to overcome funding worries, incorporating additional regional events could be a success differentiator.  

Event management software eases the administrative burden for your staff, while putting on a wow-worthy event for members.

While events are the foundation of success, member priorities are just as important. 

Member and Donor Retention Trends  

Knowledge gap 

Associations have a knowledge gap that is costing them members. Several years of association trends research show that association members consistently place much higher importance on job opportunities and career advancement than professionals recognize.    

This gap has direct financial implications because self-paying members are becoming increasingly selective about perceived organizational value and cost justification, which is the primary reason memberships lapse.    

Overcoming the Cost of Member Churn 

It costs more to acquire a new member or donor than to retain an existing one, making high retention rates paramount for organizational stability. Long-term supporters are more likely to participate, volunteer, and increase their spending or giving.  

To achieve this loyalty, associations must focus on several core strategies: personalization, recognition, consistent communication, and proactive feedback loops. 

Since high retention rates depend on scaling personalized engagement and data-driven insights, associations are increasingly relying on artificial intelligence (AI) to automate time-consuming tasks and empower staff to execute these core strategies more effectively. 

The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in Associations  

AI will become central to association success in 2026 by dramatically easing the administrative burden on your team, freeing up staff time for high-value strategic work. It is now being used to automate repetitive tasks, extract crucial insights from data, and create tailored member experiences that fundamentally change how organizations operate. 

AI Use Cases: Efficiency and Member Service 

Associations can use AI tools to streamline workflows across several key areas: 

  • Content Repurposing: Use AI writing tools to quickly adapt existing content into customized emails and social media posts for multiple audience segments, increasing engagement and saving time.  
  • Member Feedback Surveys: Generate customized surveys and use AI to categorize and analyze unstructured information from open-ended responses or CRM notes, transforming raw feedback into actionable insights, setting up your organization for better decision-making.  
  • Predictive Analytics: AI helps identify engagement trends to forecast renewals or event attendance based on past member behavior, enabling proactive intervention and strategic planning. 

AI for Association Fundraising Trends, Segmentation, and Internal Workflows 

Beyond basic automation, AI is being strategically applied to core business functions: 

  • Fundraising and Segmentation: AI tools can segment donors based on specific behaviors, predict their giving potential, and even recommend personalized outreach strategies, helping teams maximize giving and improve relationship depth. 
  • AI Data Analysis and Prediction: AI data platforms for associations that integrate machine learning help organizations identify which programs drive the most engagement or revenue growth, ensuring strategic investment decisions are data-driven. 
  • Project Management and Internal Workflows: AI-enabled tools streamline collaboration by analyzing workloads, predicting bottlenecks, and suggesting resource reallocations. This allows staff to operate with enterprise-level efficiency, enabling smaller teams to focus more on strategy and relationships. 

The rise of AI in associations is ultimately about empowering people, not replacing them. By using technology to handle time-consuming tasks and uncover deep insights, associations can operate more strategically and create greater value for every stakeholder. 

Personalization and Segmentation for Renewals 

Personalized outreach remains one of the strongest drivers of member and donor loyalty and a critical association trend for success in 2026. Associations should segment audiences by factors like engagement level, membership type, and career interests to ensure messages resonate.  

Data and automation are key to scale this approach without straining your staff, while delivering tailored renewal messages, curated event recommendations, or customized education content.  

Personalized communication ensures members feel seen and valued, leading to higher renewal and satisfaction rates. 

Recognition and Appreciation Programs 

Acknowledging the individuals and organizations that sustain your mission is essential for retention and inspiration. Recognition is one of the most powerful tools to deepen emotional connection.  

Publicly recognize top volunteers, long-term members, or high-impact donors through awards, newsletters, social media spotlights, or annual appreciation events. Even simple, authentic gestures—like handwritten thank-you notes or personalized emails from leadership—can have a lasting influence. Making recognition visible, authentic, and consistent strengthens loyalty across every segment.  

Year-Round Storytelling and Impact Communication 

Retention depends on consistent engagement beyond renewal periods or fundraising campaigns. Associations must sustain year-round communication that highlights member success, program impact, and community progress. Use storytelling—through success stories, behind-the-scenes updates, and videos—to showcase the tangible difference support makes.  

Combining these narratives with data points demonstrates measurable results and transparency. Maintaining a steady cadence of storytelling keeps your audience emotionally invested and reinforces the value of belonging. 

Listening to Member Needs 

Inviting members and donors to share opinions creates transparency and trust. Use short post-event or post-campaign surveys and feedback forms to gather actionable insights that can shape programming and benefits.  

And remember to close the feedback loop. Publicly share what you heard and the steps you are taking in response. A simple “You said, we did” update in a newsletter or meeting recap demonstrates accountability, builds credibility, and transforms passive members into active stakeholders. 

Association management software makes it simple to manage member expectations and ensure your organization is doing everything it can to maintain members, while growing your base.  

While events and meeting member expectations are paramount, uncertain economic conditions will make an impact on associations in 2026.  

Market and Economic Trends Impacting Associations  

Continued economic uncertainty and fluctuating funding conditions will continue to influence organizations in 2026.  

Political shifts, inflationary pressures, and fundamental changes in donor behavior are reshaping strategic planning. To stay resilient and identify growth opportunities, organizations will need to embrace fluidity.  

Navigating Market Disruptions and Reactive Giving 

Economic uncertainty has changed donor behaviors. Smaller, one-time donations made in response to immediate social, political, or environmental flashpoints have become commonplace, and while these bursts provide short-term revenue, they often lack the consistency needed for long-term sustainability.  

The key challenge for associations is converting this momentary engagement into sustained support. Just like with members, organizations must nurture relationships after a reactive gift to turn those one-time donors into lifelong supporters. 

The average cost of getting a new donor being 5x greater than retaining an existing one, and the Go-To Guide for Donor Retention covers how to calculate and track your retention rate, experiences to increase retention, how to segment donor data for better targeting, stewarding your relationship, and more.  

Pricing, Value, and Relevance Pressures 

Associations face increased pressure to justify their fees and membership dues. This means a constant need to re-evaluate pricing models to ensure they are perceived as fair and accessible.  

Crucially, organizations must focus on maintaining and clearly communicating their relevance and value proposition. 

 Associations that successfully position themselves as trusted, solution-driven leaders within their industries can better withstand financial pressures and convert general interest into ongoing commitment. 

Role of Storytelling and Transparency in Donor Engagement 

Creativity and storytelling have become essential tools for sustained donor and member engagement, cutting through the noise of a crowded environment. Associations should use storytelling to illustrate their impact in relatable, human terms, highlighting individual success stories and connecting them directly to mission outcomes.  

This content should be combined with a commitment to transparency and impact reporting throughout the year. Consistent and authentic communication builds trust, deepens emotional connection, and keeps members and donors engaged beyond moments of crisis or reactive giving. 

Innovation, Membership Models, and Gamification 

Innovation is essential for relevance, engagement, and sustainability. As member expectations evolve and digital transformation accelerates, associations that embrace creativity are positioned to thrive. Embracing creativity is a market differentiator and helps your organization standout from others.  

Innovation and Creativity in Association Growth 

Association leaders must create space for teams to think differently, test new ideas, and refine programs through collaboration. Without creative bandwidth, even strong strategies can stagnate.  

Organizations must foster an internal culture of innovation by encouraging cross-functional brainstorming, small pilot programs, and open feedback loops to surface new approaches. 

By empowering staff to experiment and reward innovative thinking, associations position themselves to design more adaptive and member-centered solutions. 

Experimentation and adaptive solutions are critical for all organizations, and small-staff associations are not immune to these demands for innovation; to help resource-constrained teams navigate these challenges, explore the 2025 Small Staff Associations Report.  

New Membership Models and Recurring Programs 

Membership is moving toward flexibility, personalization, and experiential value. While the traditional tiered structure remains relevant, modern members expect choices that align with their career stage, engagement level, and financial capacity.  

Source: Closing the Gap: Solving the Disconnect Around Career Prioritization 

Association members look to their organization to provide jobs, training, and advancement opportunities, along with a well-defined mix of these three, and associations are coming up short.   

Elevate career advancement programs from “nice to have” to “need to have.” Promote them as a core membership value, not just non-dues revenue drivers.  

Discover how the American College of Sports Medicine modernized their educational offerings, launched new revenue-generating courses, and created a strategic roadmap for their 50,000 learners. Read more 

Discover how the American College of Sports Medicine modernized their educational offerings, launched new revenue-generating courses, and created a strategic roadmap for their 50,000 learners.

Read more

Gamification as an Engagement Trend 

Gamification has emerged as one of the fastest-growing engagement trends globally. For associations, it represents a creative and data-driven way to make participation more interactive, enjoyable, and measurable.  

Incorporating game-based mechanics across events, trainings, and online platforms helps turn passive participation into active involvement. Gamification across events, learning, and communities can include strategies like progress trackers, leaderboards, digital badges, and interactive challenges. Gamification keeps members engaged, returning, and invested in the organization’s success. 

Major Giving and Long-Term Fundraising Trends 

With changing donor behaviors center stage, fundraising success will increasingly depend on cultivating deep, long-term relationships. Major giving remains one of the most reliable and sustainable revenue sources, but it requires patience, data-driven stewardship, and a personalized approach to secure the transformational gifts that advance missions for years to come. 

Long Donor Cultivation Timelines and Data-Driven Identification 

Major gifts rarely happen quickly; they are the result of years of relationship-building. On average, it can take 4–5 years and 18–24 personalized touchpoints to successfully obtain a major gift from a recurring donor.  

Associations can strengthen their major giving pipeline by using data-driven identification. Tracking engagement data in fundraising software helps identify high-potential donors early, ensuring resources are focused on those ready to deepen their commitment through consistent touchpoints and meaningful involvement. 

See how Littleton Public Schools Foundation transformed donor tracking.  

Stewardship, Transparency, and Sustainability 

Sustaining relationships with major donors requires intentional, ongoing stewardship that shifts the focus from simple solicitation to true partnership.  

Organizations must offer regular updates, and provide sincere, appropriate recognition to the donor. By fostering genuine partnerships through personalized experiences and accountability, associations can secure financial stability and turn one-time major supporters into lifelong advocates. 

Learn how St. Anthony Catholic School transformed donor engagement and realized revitalized success.

Read the Case Study

Creative Fundraising Ideas 

Associations can supplement revenue through diverse fundraising initiatives throughout the year.  

Creative ideas include peer-to-peer elements for galas, such as "of the year" fundraising champions or table captains, and online hosted auctions featuring member-specific items like training courses.  

Organizations can also leverage virtual community events, such as scavenger hunts or 5Ks driven by a junior board, or quickly implement text-to-donate campaigns for specific funding needs.  

Finally, the simple act of turning on recurring giving options on existing donation pages can drastically increase donor retention. 

If you’re looking for other things to add to or adapt for your fundraising calendar, read our 30+ Revenue Sources Workbook today to further unlock growth potential.

Key Takeaways for Association Leaders in 2026 

2026 is the year of blending technology with a refreshed focus on member value. Focusing on these four association trends will help associations navigate economic uncertainty and meet evolving member expectations in the new year. 

  • Adopt a Member-First Mindset and Elevate Career Value 

Associations must urgently address the knowledge gap regarding member priorities, recognizing that members consistently place high importance on job opportunities and career advancement.  

Organizations must elevate career programs to a core membership benefit. True stability depends on fostering a member-first mindset where every strategy, from events to education, is designed to clearly reinforce the personal and professional value of belonging.  

  •  Embrace Innovation and Scale Engagement with Technology 

The need to scale personalization and streamline administrative processes makes innovation and tech adoption mandatory. AI tools will streamline associations in 2026. From automating repetitive tasks, to extracting crucial data insights. This strategic adoption of AI empowers staff to execute high-value strategies without being overwhelmed, directly supporting retention goals and maximizing efficiency. 

  • Strengthen Collaboration and Strategic Partnerships 

To succeed in a climate defined by staffing constraints and economic shifts, associations must eliminate internal silos and increase collaboration across functions and partners. Collaboration ensures the organization can be nimbler, respond to feedback, and deliver a seamless member experience. 

  • Balance Short-Term Adaptability with Long-Term Planning 

Success in 2026 requires balancing short-term adaptability, such as navigating reactive giving trends and experimenting with regional events, with resilient long-term planning. Organizations must use data to influence and shape strategy. Creating new revenue streams through fundraising provides a foundation of predictability that stabilizes the organization against external economic shocks. 

Maximize Your Impact in 2026 

The association trends of 2026 clearly emphasize that a proactive, member-centric, and data-driven approach is essential for achieving long-term sustainability. By embracing AI, addressing the career value gap, and prioritizing consistent engagement, your organization can turn challenges into opportunities. 

Don’t let outdated systems slow your progress. Explore how Association Management Software provides the unified data environment and integrated tools needed to streamline operations, execute complex retention strategies, and foster an innovative culture. 

FAQ

item.title: Planning Large Scale Events vs Small Scale Events
item.dc:creator: dan
item.content:encoded:
Planning an event and watching it unfold is a great experience. But when that event is on a larger scale, there are many more elements to consider throughout the planning process. Planning large-scale events requires greater attention to detail and an understanding of the different aspects of event planning. It also requires the confidence that everything is being managed efficiently.
When you’re planning an event, regardless of its size, having a plan and timeline is essential. Thus, we've compiled an event planning checklist to help you stay organized and on track throughout the planning process. From large-scale event planning to each factor that will make your events successful, we’re here to help you every step of the way.

Types of Large-Scale Events

To give you an idea, here are some examples of large-scale events that require a lot of planning, resources, and attention:

  • New Product Launches: This is when a company unveils a new product or service to the public. Usually, these tend to be large-scale events with a great deal of hype and fanfare.
  • Industry Conferences: This can be anything from a digital media conference to an electronics conference, or even a medical conference.
  • Global Company Holiday Parties: These are festive events that companies often throw to celebrate the holidays with their employees.
  • Professional Seminars: Professional seminars are events that provide insight into a particular industry. They may focus on the latest trends in technology, innovations in marketing, or even motivational talks for entrepreneurs.

Types of Small-Scale Events

Small-scale events tend to require fewer resources and planning than large-scale events. Here are some examples of small-scale events that can be planned in a relatively short amount of time:

  • Birthday Parties: More often than not, birthday parties are usually celebrated on a small scale.
  • Family Reunions: These usually only involve immediate family and close friends, so they tend to be smaller events.
  • Corporate Training Seminars: Corporate training seminars are typically hosted on a small scale in order to bring together a select group of professionals for specialized training.
  • Community Events: Community events can range from small-scale fundraisers to art shows.
  • Dinner Party: Common with friends and family, dinner parties are often small and intimate.

Differences Between Planning Large Scale Events vs Small Scale Events

With the mutual goal of successfully hosting an event, large-scale and small-scale events require different approaches when it comes to the planning process.

Budget
Budgeting for a large-scale event is often more complex considering that there are usually more people involved, which means higher costs for food, venues, and entertainment. On the other hand, smaller events usually require less budgeting since there are fewer people to cater to. Make sure to consider the budget at the earliest stage of event planning in order to plan accordingly.
Event Logistics
Large-scale event logistics involve things like transportation, lodging, and security. Small-scale events may not require these types of logistics because they are smaller in size. Furthermore, large-scale events may require more extensive communication and coordination with different vendors and partners.
Management
Event planners that handle large-scale events need to be highly organized and able to multitask. A larger staff is also required for such events. Smaller-scale events may not require such high levels of management since there are fewer people involved.Oftentimes, however, micromanagement is still necessary. After all, a successful event doesn’t just depend on the big picture — it’s the small details that make an event come together.
Email Sending or Promotion
Promoting large-scale events can often be done through social media, particularly if there are limited funds. This can be anything from creating ads to using influencers for maximum exposure. Small-scale events may be promoted through word-of-mouth or other online channels. Emails are also used in large-scale events. However, if you choose this option, make sure to keep the emails brief and catchy. Use an automated tool to help you send out bulk emails for maximum reach.
Venue
Choosing a venue for large-scale events requires a great deal of research and planning. From hotels to convention centers, there are many factors to consider. This is especially true for companies that have executives and/or professional attendees who expect a higher standard. On the other hand, small-scale events might require less extensive research since they usually take place in more intimate settings. This usually takes a day or two for the planner to finalize.
Staffing
An event manager would need to hire more personnel when planning a large-scale event. This includes different contractors and vendors, as well as security staff. Smaller events don’t require the same amount of staffing since they are usually not as involved. Depending on the venue, some staff may be provided by the host. In some cases like restaurant dinner parties, you don't need to hire additional staff.
Timeframe
With all things considered, the timeframe for planning a large-scale event is often longer than that of smaller events. The complexity and number of details involved require more time to solidify all aspects. On the other hand, small-scale events may be planned in a shorter time frame due to their limited scope.

Main Benefits of Each Type of Event

Now that you have a better understanding of the differences between large and small-scale events, let's talk about some benefits of each.
Reasons to Organize a Large-Scale Event

  1. Raise awareness and visibility for a cause or organization: A large-scale event can be a great way to draw attention to a particular cause or organization, especially if it aligns with the values of the event. This could be a charity fundraiser, a political rally, or a cultural festival. By organizing a large-scale event, you can generate media coverage and reach a wide audience with your message.
  2. Foster community and connection: Large-scale events can bring people together in a way that small gatherings or individual activities cannot. They can be a great way to foster a sense of community and belonging among attendees, as well as to connect with new people who share similar interests. This is especially important in times when social isolation and disconnection are common.
  3. Create opportunities for networking and professional development: A large-scale event can be a great opportunity for attendees to network with each other and build new connections. This can be especially valuable for professionals looking to expand their network or gain new skills and knowledge. Many large-scale events feature speakers or workshops focused on specific industries or topics, providing attendees with valuable learning opportunities.
  4. Enhance brand reputation and credibility: For businesses or organizations, a well-executed large-scale event can be a great way to enhance their reputation and credibility. It can showcase their expertise and capabilities, as well as demonstrate their commitment to their customers or stakeholders. A successful large-scale event can also generate positive media coverage and word-of-mouth promotion, which can further boost a brand's reputation.

Reasons to Organize a Small-Scale Event

  1. More intimate and personal: Small-scale events can be more intimate and personal, allowing attendees to get to know each other and create deeper connections. It can also be a great way to create a more personalized and memorable experience for attendees.
  2. More manageable and cost-effective: Small-scale events are generally easier to manage and less expensive to organize than large-scale events. This can be a great option for businesses or organizations with limited resources or those just starting out. It can also be a good choice for events that are focused on a specific target audience, as it allows for more targeted marketing and outreach efforts.
  3. Greater flexibility and adaptability: Small-scale events offer more flexibility and adaptability than large-scale events. If something goes wrong or if last-minute changes need to be made, it is generally easier to pivot and make adjustments when working with a smaller group of attendees.
  4. More focus on the event experience: Because small-scale events are generally more intimate and personal, they can be a great opportunity to really focus on the event experience. You can pay closer attention to the details and create a more immersive and engaging atmosphere for attendees.
Tips for Planning Large-Scale Events and Small-Scale Events

With their differences, organizing large or small-scale events often follows the same basic planning process. Here are some tips for planning both types of events:
Set Clear Goals and Objectives
Before you get started, it is important to have a clear understanding of what you want to achieve with your event. This will help keep you focused throughout the planning process and ensure that all of your efforts are directed toward meeting your goals. Here are some questions you need to answer:

  • What's the purpose of your event?
  • How much money do you want to make?
  • How many tickets do you need to sell?
  • What kind of experience do you want to create for attendees?

Set Your Budget
Budget is arguably one of the most important aspects of event planning since it will dictate many of your decisions. Start by determining what you have to work with and then allocate funds for various elements like venue, catering, programming, marketing, and staffing.
You can use your previous events handled and from there, you can create a realistic budget that will help you stay on track. If any gap is found in the budget, feel free to take educated guesses and estimates to fill it in.
Secure the Necessary Resources
Once you have set your budget, you need to start looking for the necessary resources and suppliers that will help you bring your event vision to life. This process may involve finding a venue, booking entertainment or speakers, organizing catering services, and more. Additional considerations are WiFi connections, audio and video equipment, power supply, and other tech-related needs.
Choose a Date and Time
The planning timeline plays a critical role in the success of an event. Choose a date and time that will work for most attendees and give you enough time to make all necessary preparations. Keep in mind important holidays, local events, or other potential conflicts when deciding on dates. Make sure to take into account the day of the week and time of day when selecting the date and time for your event.
Search for Sponsors
Sponsors can be a great way to generate additional funds for your events. Depending on the size and scope of your event, you may be able to secure one or more sponsors. Reach out to local businesses and offer them various packages and benefits in exchange for their support. Make a list of those who are likely to be interested in sponsoring your event and start contacting them. The market rate of your sponsorship packages should be based on the size of your event and potential reach.

Plan and Manage Large or Small Scale Events With Attendee Interactive
Event planning can be overwhelming but at the same time gives a sense of fulfillment. The key to success is having the right tools and technology. Attendee Interactive is the perfect platform for managing both large and small-scale events. From collecting abstract submissions and reviewing sessions to publishing final agendas and managing CE credit, Attendee Interactive makes it easy to handle all aspects of your event in one place.

Feel free to contact us today and request a demo to further explore our powerful suite of event planning tools.

Request demo

item.title: Event Marketing: Definition, Types, Strategies, and Best Practices
item.dc:creator: dan
item.content:encoded:
Events are the lifeblood of mission-driven organizations. From accounting for most of an association’s revenue, to keeping members informed and engaged, events are crucial to success, but where would an organization be without effective event marketing?.  

Event marketing provides organizations a unique opportunity to engage their audience on a personal level, generate buzz, and achieve significant outcomes. Whether it’s a product launch, a trade show, or an interactive virtual event, the right event marketing strategy can have a lasting impact.   

Discover how technological advancements can be the key to successful event-driven marketing strategies that can elevate your brand, enhance customer engagement, and achieve your organizational goals. 

What Is Event Marketing? 

Event marketing involves creating, promoting, and executing events to promote an experience or service. These events can range from large-scale trade shows and conferences to intimate workshops and webinars.  

The primary goal of event marketing is to engage the audience directly, create memorable experiences, and foster deeper connections between attendees and the brand.   

Event marketing, also known as “experiential marketing,” focuses on creating immersive, engaging experiences for consumers, enabling them to interact with a brand’s products or services in memorable ways.  

Event marketing combines all facets of marketing from advertisements and e-mail campaigns to increase brand awareness, generate leads, create attendee loyalty, and enhance brand perception. 

Benefits of Event Marketing 

Event marketing not only drives immediate results in terms of leads and sales but also nurtures long-term relationships and enhances brand recognition, making it a valuable component of a comprehensive marketing strategy.   

Event marketing can significantly impact an organization’s marketing strategy through:   

  • Increased Brand Awareness: Events provide a platform to showcase your brand, products, or services directly to a targeted audience.   
  • Lead Generation: Attendees already have a genuine interest in the industry or product.  
  • Customer Loyalty and Relationship Building: Personalized interactions at events help strengthen relationships with existing customers, enhancing loyalty and encouraging repeat business.  
  • Market Research and Feedback: Events offer opportunities to gather valuable insights through attendee feedback and interactions.  
  • Thought Leadership and Industry Influence: Hosting or participating in industry events positions your organization as a thought leader, enhancing credibility and influencing perceptions within your industry.  
  • Networking and Partnerships: Events facilitate networking with industry peers and potential partners, creating opportunities for collaborations and business growth.  
  • Measurable Return on Investment (ROI): Event marketing allows for clear measurement of ROI through metrics such as lead conversions, sales generated, and attendee satisfaction.   

Types of Event Marketing 

Event marketing is not one-size-fits-all; the format you choose dictates how you will engage your audience and the type of technology required to support it. No matter what type of event your organization is hosting, event management software makes it simple to plan, promote, and execute your event.  

In-Person Events 

Traditional face-to-face gatherings remain the gold standard for events and high-touch networking. These events allow for up-close interaction with products and create deep, personal connections. 

Virtual Events 

Hosted entirely online, virtual events, including webinars and digital summits, offer maximum accessibility and cost-efficiency. They allow organizations to reach a large audience without the overhead of travel or venues, providing a wealth of digital data to track attendee behavior and interests. 

Hybrid Events 

Hybrid events combine a live, in-person gathering with a digital component, allowing remote and local attendees to interact simultaneously. This "best of both worlds" approach maximizes reach while maintaining the prestige and intimacy of an on-site experience.  

Community & Experiential Events 

These events focus on "experience over information," often involving immersive activations or local community workshops. The goal is to create a lasting emotional bond by allowing consumers to interact with your organization in a creative, hands-on environment. 

Event Marketing Strategies 

Your event’s succes depends on the strategy behind it. High- impact events start by identifying goals, planning logistics, promoting the event, and determining how to measure success. Your strategy should aim to enhance brand awareness, generate leads, foster customer relationships, and determine what specific business objectives your event will accomplish.  

Selecting the right type of event marketing is critical to achieving your business objectives and resonating with your target audience.  

These are some of the criteria to consider when developing your event marketing strategy:  

  • Organizational Goals: Determine whether the goal is to generate leads, increase brand awareness, foster customer loyalty, etc.  
  • Target Audience: Understand the preferences and behaviors of your target audience and select an event type that effectively engages them.   
  • Budget: Assess the available budget and allocate resources accordingly. Some events require significant investment, while others can be more cost-effective.  
  • Resources and Capabilities: Consider internal resources you can leverage for your event, such as staff expertise and availability. Determine what outside resources you’ll need for your event, such as additional speakers, immersive experiences, and more.  
  • Geographical Reach: Decide the scope of your event.  

See how The American Society of Neuroradiology leveraged Momentive Event Management Software to save staff time, stay organized, and reduce redundancies in their event planning and management. 

Event Logistics and Attendee Engagement  

Before you begin promoting your event, your organization will need to figure out the event agenda, and what sort of opportunities you’ll have for attendee engagement. High engagement levels are the primary indicator of a successful event experience, are essential for retention, and provide promotion opportunities.  

Our whitepaper on Prioritizing the Attendee Experience helps future-proof your conferences. 

Event Q&A 

Breaking the "fourth wall" between speakers and the audience is vital. Use dedicated Q&A sessions to let attendees voice their questions. For virtual or hybrid events, digital Q&A tools enable remote participants to submit questions in real -time, ensuring they feel just as involved as those in the room. 

Live Polling 

Live polling provides instant interaction and valuable data. By asking the audience to vote on a topic or share their opinion during a session, you keep them focused and active. This real-time feedback can also help speakers pivot their presentations to better suit the audience's interests. 

Networking 

Facilitated networking is often the top reason people attend events. Designate specific times and "zones" for attendees to connect. Whether through "speed networking" sessions, dedicated networking sessions, or casual mixers, the goal is to help your members build the professional relationships they value. 

Gamification 

Turn engagement into a friendly competition to drive specific behaviors through event gamification. By using a leaderboard to reward attendees for visiting sponsor booths, attending sessions, or participating in polls, you increase overall engagement activity. Gamification makes the event more memorable and ensures that even the "quiet" areas of your trade show floor see consistent traffic. 

Pre-event Marketing 

To ensure high attendance and build anticipation, your promotion strategy must be multi-channel and consistent. Effective pre-event marketing creates FOMO that drives early registrations. 

E-mail Campaigns 

E-mail remains the most direct way to reach your audience. Use segmented lists to send personalized invitations, offer "early bird" discounts, and regularly communicate logistical updates with attendees.. Automation allows you to trigger e-mails based on member behavior, ensuring the right message reaches the right inbox at the right time. 

Event management software takes the guesswork out of attendee engagement, with automated tools from registration to event day check-in.  

Social Media Marketing 

Social media is an easy way to promote the event to your audience, while reaching additional interested attendees. Create a unique event hashtag and use it across all posts to centralize the conversation. Consider creating teaser videos, speaker reveals, and "behind-the-scenes" content to drive FOMO and increase registrations. 

TIP:

Encourage your followers to share the event by offering registration giveaways or branded swagto reach additional interested audiences.

Content Marketing 

Continue to create buzz by publishing blog posts, whitepapers, or even podcast episodes related to your event’s core themes. Content shows your dedication to providing high- value experiences on the day of your event and highlights your organization's expertise throughout.  

Influencer Partnerships 

Collaborate with industry experts or key opinion leaders to expand your reach. When a trusted figure in your field promotes your event or confirms their attendance, it adds instant credibility. Provide these partners with custom graphics and discount codes to make it easy for them to share the event with their unique audiences, while providing your organization with the tracking tools needed to measure effectiveness.  

Post-Event Marketing & Follow-Up 

Successful event promotion lays the foundation for a successful event, but the marketing cycle does not end when the final session wraps up.  

What your organization does after an event is critical to growing relationships and turning event momentum into long-term organizational growth. 

These four strategies can turn a one-time attendee into a lifelong fan:  

Recap/Follow-Up E-mails  

Send a "thank you" e-mail within 24 to 48 hours while the experience is still fresh. Include links to session recordings, slide decks, or a key takeaways summary. For those who couldn't attend, send a separate "sorry we missed you" recap to maintain their interest in future events. 

If you create any content from event sessions, be sure to include these in your e-mails.  

Post-Event Surveys 

To improve future events, it’s essential to gather attendee feedback. Event management software makes it easy to send post-event surveys to measure attendee satisfaction regarding speakers, venue, and overall value. Keep surveys brief and consider offering a small incentive, such as a discount for next year’s registration, to drive participation rates.  

Lead Nurturing 

After your event, it’s time to start nurturing and building relationships. Segment your attendee list based on the sessions they attended or the booths they visited. Provide tailored follow-up content that addresses their specific interests to take them from a one-time attendee to a lifelong fan.  

Post-Event Evaluations 

While surveying attendees, it’s also important to ask your team how the event went. Compare the actual outcomes against the original organizational goals, discuss what worked, what logistical hurdles arose, and how your team thought the event went.  

Encourage candid discussions and take the feedback to heart. Combine your team’s feedback with the attendees’ feedback and use that as the basis for your event marketing strategy for next year.  

Measuring Event Marketing Success 

Measuring success requires looking at both quantitative numbers and qualitative data. Just as you analyze employee and attendee surveys, this data will shape your future events.

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) 

Track specific metrics that align with your initial goals. Common event KPIs include: 

  • Total Registration vs. Actual Attendance: Measures the "drop-off" rate. 
  • Session Engagement: Which topics kept people in their seats (or logged in)? 
  • Social Media Reach: The volume of posts and engagement using your event hashtag. 
  • Sponsor and Exhibitor ROI: Measure success for your partners by tracking booth traffic, lead scans, and sponsored session attendance.  
  • Net Promoter Score: A post-event metric that asks attendees how likely they are to recommend the event to a colleague.   
  • Cost Per Attendee: Calculated by dividing the total event budget by the number of attendees.   

Determining ROI 

Calculate the Return on Investment by comparing the total event cost (venue, tech, marketing) against the revenue generated from ticket sales, sponsorships, and new memberships. 

Beyond financials, consider Learning ROI, the measurable change in attendee knowledge or competence. Learning ROI is largely calculated through feedback collected from attendee surveys.  

Building Better Events: Mastering the Marketing Lifecycle 

Effective event marketing is far more than a pre-show checklist; it is a continuous lifecycle that transforms logistics into a high-performance engine for organizational growth.  

By integrating strategic promotion with immersive on-site engagement and data-driven follow-up, mission-driven organizations can bridge the gap between simple logistical efficiency and unforgettable attendee experiences.  

Mastering this cycle ensures that your events deliver lasting value to members and measurable results to your bottom line. 

How to master your event strategy: 

  • Sustainable Growth: Maximizing reach through hybrid capabilities and multi-channel promotion to secure a diverse, global audience. 
  • Deepened Loyalty: Moving beyond passive attendance to active participation through personalized engagement and networking. 
  • Data-Driven Intelligence: Using real-time analytics and AMS integration to inform year-round retention and marketing strategies. 
  • Optimized Revenue: Providing sponsors with qualified lead data and proving high ROI to secure recurring financial partnerships. 

When you move from simply managing an event to mastering its strategic impact, you solidify your organization’s role as an indispensable industry leader. 

Ready to transform your association's events into high-performance marketing engines? Request a Demo of our Event Management Software today. 

FAQ

item.title: Five Things Your Association Can Automate Right Now
item.dc:creator: dan
item.content:encoded:
The concept of “automation” can seem somewhat intimidating. Even in today’s tech-driven world, it can prompt people to ask: “Is a computer going to take over my job?” 

But to deliver a great member experience, there’s always more to do. Automation reduces the burden of manual tasks – saving association staff time and effort that can be directed toward higher-value work. 

It’s time to embrace automation. Here’s how you can start today. Get ready to work more efficiently and have more time to deliver a great member experience. Here are five things your association can automate now: 
 

1. Email Communications To Members 

Automate email communications based on members’ actions, such as registering for an event or paying membership dues. For example, when members join your organization and pay their annual dues, they can receive an automated email confirmation of their membership, which includes a receipt of payment. 

Another example: Your organization can create a welcome campaign that’s automatically triggered when a new member joins. The campaign can help familiarize new members with your organization and encourage them to take advantage of the benefits offered by your association immediately. 

TECH TIP:

Nimble Create is a visual template builder included with Nimble AMS, allowing you to easily build templates (no coding required) for branded, personalized, and information-rich content. Use it to automatically pull member data and other details directly from your Nimble AMS system into your email content, providing a more personalized member experience.

2. Membership Renewals 

The Momentive Software Member Loyalty Study found that 13 percent of lapsed members simply forgot to renew their membership. A simple yet powerful way to address this issue is to offer an option to automatically renew membership, with the membership fee charged to the member’s credit card.

TECH TIP:

Nimble AMS allows you to set up this option, with corresponding automated emails that notify members when their membership has been renewed.

3. Member Guidance 

Your members’ needs and expectations evolve as they move through their careers and their membership with your organization. Support their needs throughout the member journey by guiding the member experience through intelligent chatbots, suggested next steps, and products. 
 

For example, much like the familiar Amazon-like shopping experience, you can recommend new product purchases based on past behaviors and “what other members like you purchased”. This has the added benefit of increasing your non-dues revenues. 

TECH TIP:

Nimble AMS uses Salesforce Einstein artificial intelligence (AI) technology, enabling point-and-click AI. Using this technology, you can easily make predictions and provide automated, intelligent guidance to members.

4. Member Service 

Chatbots have begun to capture the attention of associations as a means to provide member services more efficiently. You’ve probably come across chatbots multiple times on websites when a box pops up and asks if you need help. 
 

Using chatbots, you can automate aspects of member service, such as answering/assisting with common questions to provide immediate response 24/7 and free up staff time. With chatbots intercepting and answering more straightforward questions, the more complex inquiries can be passed to a live agent, who can take time to assist the member and ensure an exceptional experience. 

TECH TIP:

Using Nimble AMS, you can easily set up AI-based chatbots with no coding required.

Another way to provide excellent member service more efficiently is by automating other routine, manual tasks. With workflow automation technology, you set up business rules in software that automatically route data and files between people and systems, streamlining processes and improving efficiency. Your staff can work more efficiently and spend more time creating a great experience throughout the member journey. 

TECH TIP:

Nimble AMS leverages Salesforce Process Builder to allow your staff to save valuable time by automating complex business processes. Without code, your staff can specify process criteria to automate tasks such as automatically updating or creating new records, emails, and tasks, or submitting approval requests – all in a few simple steps.

5. Tax Processing 

Tax processing might not seem exciting when it comes to providing a great member experience. However, automating it allows you to process taxes more easily, reducing the manual burden, eliminating human error, and saving association staff time and effort that can be directed elsewhere toward serving members. 

TECH TIP:

Nimble AMS supports automated, complex tax processing, enabling associations to manage taxes both nationally and internationally easily.

Take The Next Step

There are many ways automation can help your association. With the right technology, your organization can become more efficient and deliver a better member experience. 

Discover how Nimble AMS can help your organization bring AI and process automation to life for your association.

Schedule a demo.

item.title: Help Your Members Advance Their Careers with Career Insights
item.dc:creator: dan
item.content:encoded:
Your association is a go-to resource for your industry. So, your members naturally turn to your association for help with planning and growing their careers. In fact, association trends research from Momentive Software shows that help with career development is consistently among the most important benefits to members.

There are lots of general career websites out there today. However, your association has a unique opportunity to provide your members with resources specific to your industry or profession that can help with their career advancement.
One powerful way to provide career development resources for your members is to offer an online career planning portal that includes career insights for every role in your industry.

Why should you provide career insights for your association members?

Providing career insights through a career planning portal gives your members a convenient way to access the information they need to make informed decisions about their careers. It gives them details about positions in your association’s field – including things like salary data, job responsibilities, job growth opportunities, and educational requirements – all in one location.

This type of resource can help your members throughout the entire career journey. For example:

  • Students and new graduates can gain information about various positions in your industry to help them decide what types of jobs might be a good fit.
  • Career changers can better understand what a new job might entail so that they can be sure it’s the change they want to make.
  • Remote workers can determine which next career step might be more (or less) appropriate for at-home work and their overall career goals.
  • Unemployed professionals can gain a more thorough understanding of a position they might be targeting. Those who are considering relocating can also learn more about specific occupations in certain locations.
  • Professionals returning to the workplace can assess if they have the right skills and meet the requirements for a specific position.
  • Late-stage careerists can get a clearer view of job outlooks that can help them decide if (or when) it’s time to retire or make a career change.

How can your association provide members with career insights?

YM Careers makes it easy to provide your members with career insights. YM Careers offers job board software to power your online job board, and a Career Planning Portal is included with every YM Careers job board.

Career Planning Portal is an online career portal that provides professionals in your industry with a comprehensive set of career resources, including a Career Insights page.

With Career Insights, your members can access valuable data about every role in your industry. Here are examples of the types of information available through Career Insights:

  • Typical wages and compensation for the role
  • Job outlook – for example, projected employment date and how likely there are to be new job opportunities for the position
  • Examples of tasks the person in such a role might complete in a day
  • A “day-in-the-life” video
  • Average education level attained by people in the position
  • Subject areas that might need to be mastered for the role
  • Skills and strengths that might be needed for the role
  • Currently available job opportunities in the role
  • Other related positions

Learn more about providing career insights for your association members

As your members turn to your association to help them with career development, be sure to give them tools and resources, such as Career Insights, to help them make more informed career decisions and optimize every career stage.
item.excerpt:encoded: Learn how to give your association’s members the valuable career insights they need to make more informed career decisions.
